title: Cloud, AWS & Serverless
description: Lambda, SQS, DynamoDB, API Gateway, cost/scaling/cold starts.

cards.front: What is serverless, and what are the trade-offs?
cards.back: Running code without managing servers — the provider handles provisioning, scaling, patching; you deploy functions and pay per invocation + compute time, scaling to zero when idle. Pros: no infra management, fine-grained auto-scaling, pay-for-use, fast to ship. Cons: cold starts, execution limits (Lambda 15 min), statelessness, costlier at sustained high volume, vendor lock-in.
distractors: Serverless means there are no servers involved at all; code runs in the browser.
You pay a fixed monthly fee regardless of usage, and it never scales to zero.
Serverless functions keep local state between invocations, so no external store is needed.
It is always cheaper than containers, including at sustained high steady load.
Serverless is best for long-running heavy jobs because there are no execution limits.
You manage the operating system and patching while the provider writes the code.
cards.quizAnswer: Run code without managing servers, paying per use and scaling to zero; trade-offs include cold starts, limits, and statelessness.

cards.front: What causes Lambda cold starts and how do you mitigate them?
cards.back: A cold start happens when no warm environment exists, so AWS downloads code, starts the runtime, inits the container, and runs your init code before handling the request — adding latency. Mitigate with Provisioned Concurrency, smaller packages/fewer deps, lighter runtimes/lazy imports, moving heavy init to module scope (reused across warm invocations), and right-sizing memory. For async pipelines, cold starts often don't matter.
distractors: Cold starts happen when the function runs too often and the CPU overheats.
They are caused by large response payloads; returning less data eliminates them.
Provisioned Concurrency makes cold starts worse by keeping containers idle.
Moving initialization inside the handler (re-run every call) avoids cold starts.
Cold starts only occur on the very first deployment and never again afterward.
Reducing the function's memory speeds up cold starts because less RAM is allocated.
cards.quizAnswer: Latency when AWS spins up a new environment; mitigate with provisioned concurrency, smaller packages, and module-scope init.

cards.front: Why put SQS in front of Lambda instead of invoking Lambda directly?
cards.back: Load leveling/backpressure (the queue absorbs bursts so spikes don't overwhelm downstream or hit concurrency limits; Lambda drains at a controlled rate), decoupling and resilience (messages wait safely if a consumer is down), built-in retries + DLQ, and fast async acknowledgment so the producer doesn't wait on processing. Direct invocation couples producer to consumer availability with no buffer.
distractors: SQS makes Lambda run faster by caching the function's code between invocations.
Direct invocation is impossible; Lambda can only be triggered through a queue.
SQS removes the need for retries because it guarantees exactly-once delivery.
It lets the producer block until processing finishes, ensuring synchronous results.
SQS in front reduces cost by skipping the Lambda invocation entirely for duplicates.
It is purely for ordering; SQS provides no buffering or backpressure benefit.
cards.quizAnswer: The queue buffers bursts, decouples producer from consumer, and adds retries/DLQ and a fast async acknowledgment.

cards.front: DynamoDB — when do you use it, and how do you model data?
cards.back: A managed NoSQL key-value/document store with single-digit-ms latency at any scale. Use for high-throughput, known-access-pattern workloads (sessions, lookups, idempotency/dedup, high-write events) — not ad-hoc analytics or joins. Modeling: design the table around access patterns first; partition key must spread load (avoid hot partitions), sort key enables ranges, GSIs/single-table design support multiple patterns, no joins — denormalize.
distractors: Use DynamoDB for complex ad-hoc analytical queries and multi-table joins.
Model it like SQL: normalize fully first, then write joins across tables at query time.
The partition key should funnel all traffic to one partition for consistency.
DynamoDB supports joins natively, so you can keep data normalized as in Postgres.
Choose the partition key last, after the data is loaded, based on query frequency.
It is a relational database with ACID transactions across arbitrary tables.
cards.quizAnswer: A managed NoSQL store; design around access patterns first, spread load with a good partition key, denormalize, no joins.

cards.front: DynamoDB vs. a relational DB — how do you choose?
cards.back: Relational (Postgres/RDS): strong consistency, ACID, flexible ad-hoc queries, joins, normalized data — choose when access patterns evolve or you need complex queries/transactional integrity (payments, orders), and it's where pgvector lives. DynamoDB: massive scale, predictable low latency, simple known access patterns, pay-per-use. It's about access patterns and consistency, not 'NoSQL is modern' — many systems use both.
distractors: Always pick DynamoDB; relational databases cannot scale and are obsolete.
Choose relational only when you need low latency; DynamoDB is for complex joins.
DynamoDB gives ACID transactions across tables, so it replaces relational entirely.
Pick based on which is newer; NoSQL is always the more modern, better choice.
Relational databases can't do transactions, so use DynamoDB for payments.
Never use both in one system; mixing data stores is always an anti-pattern.
cards.quizAnswer: Relational for ACID, joins, and ad-hoc queries; DynamoDB for scale and known access patterns. Choose by access patterns, often both.

cards.front: How do you control cloud cost?
cards.back: Right-size Lambda memory/CPU, use serverless/scale-to-zero for spiky and provisioned/containers for sustained load (the crossover is real), S3 lifecycle tiers (Standard→IA→Glacier), DynamoDB on-demand vs. provisioned capacity, caching (ElastiCache/CloudFront). For AI: cache embeddings/query results, right-size the model, control context length, batch — cost is tokens × model size. Tag resources and use Cost Explorer/budgets/alarms.
distractors: Always over-provision memory; more RAM is free and never affects cost.
Use serverless for everything, including sustained high load, to minimize cost.
Keep all S3 data in the most expensive tier so it is never accidentally deleted.
For AI cost, always use the largest frontier model since it finishes faster.
Disable monitoring and tagging to avoid the overhead cost of observability.
Cache nothing, since caches duplicate data and double your storage bill.
cards.quizAnswer: Right-size compute, scale to zero for spiky load, tier storage, cache, right-size models, and monitor spend with budgets.

cards.front: How do you handle secrets and security in the cloud?
cards.back: Never hardcode credentials — use Secrets Manager / SSM Parameter Store (or runtime env-var injection) with rotation. Apply IAM least privilege (each service gets only the permissions it needs, scoped to specific resources/actions), encryption in transit (TLS) and at rest (KMS), VPC/network isolation for sensitive components, and audit/logging via CloudTrail + CloudWatch.
distractors: Hardcode credentials in the deployment package so they're always available.
Give every Lambda full admin IAM permissions to avoid permission errors.
Skip encryption at rest since the cloud provider's data centers are physically secure.
Store secrets in plaintext environment variables committed to the repo.
Expose internal services publicly so they're easy to reach, then rely on obscurity.
Rotate credentials never, since rotation risks breaking running services.
cards.quizAnswer: Never hardcode credentials; use a secrets manager, least-privilege IAM, encryption in transit/at rest, and network isolation.

cards.front: OpenShift / containers — where do they fit vs. serverless?
cards.back: OpenShift is an enterprise Kubernetes platform for container orchestration. Reach for it (vs. Lambda) for long-running services, predictable sustained load (cheaper than per-invocation at scale), more runtime/networking control, stateful workloads, or to avoid Lambda's execution limits. Rule: serverless for event-driven/bursty/glue, containers for long-running/steady/control-heavy. Real systems mix both.
distractors: OpenShift is a serverless platform that scales to zero faster than Lambda.
Use containers only for bursty event-driven glue, and Lambda for long-running services.
OpenShift is a database engine, not a container orchestration platform.
Containers are always cheaper than serverless, including for rare bursty workloads.
Kubernetes/OpenShift cannot run stateful workloads, so it replaces Lambda exactly.
There is never a reason to mix the two; pick one platform for the whole system.
cards.quizAnswer: Containers for long-running, steady, or control-heavy services; serverless for event-driven, bursty glue. Real systems mix both.
